suggestions.name: SectionOutlinePaintRegistry
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive paint management system that maps section keys to outline paints with intelligent lookup and auto-population capabilities. Extracting them into a registry improves separation of concerns by isolating paint mapping logic from the main plotting responsibilities.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods collectively manage the outline paint properties for pie chart sections. They handle getting, setting, clearing, and looking up outline paints for specific keys, as well as managing a default outline paint. The lookup method includes auto-population logic that can fetch paints from a drawing supplier when needed.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
All members belong to the "section outline paint mapping" domain. They represent a specialized map-like structure that associates keys with Paint objects for section outlines, with fallback mechanisms and auto-population capabilities. This is essentially a paint registry or cache with intelligent lookup behavior.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates paint mapping logic from the broader PiePlot concerns
- Code reusability: This paint mapping pattern could be reused for other visual properties (fill paints, strokes, etc.)
- Complexity reduction: Removes paint management complexity from the main plot class
- Testability: Paint mapping logic can be tested independently of plot rendering

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to create a specialized Registry. The extracted class implements a "Registry" or "Map with defaults" pattern, providing a clean abstraction for managing paint associations with automatic fallback and population behavior.

suggestions.name: SectionStrokeRegistry
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive registry that manages the mapping of keys to stroke objects with default fallback and auto-population capabilities. Extracting them creates a reusable component that encapsulates stroke lookup logic and reduces the complexity of the main plotting class.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods collectively manage section outline strokes for pie chart sections. They provide a complete CRUD interface for storing, retrieving, updating, and clearing stroke configurations mapped to keys, along with default stroke management and auto-population capabilities.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
All members belong to the "stroke mapping" domain - they represent a specialized map-like data structure that associates keys with Stroke objects, provides fallback to defaults, and includes auto-population logic. This is essentially a smart registry or repository for stroke configurations.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates stroke mapping logic from the broader plotting concerns
- Code reusability: This stroke mapping pattern could be reused for other visual attributes (paints, fonts, etc.)
- Complexity reduction: Removes 6 methods and 2 fields from the God class, simplifying PiePlot
- Testability: Stroke mapping logic can be unit tested independently of chart rendering

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to create a specialized Registry. The extracted class will have high cohesion (all methods deal with stroke mapping) and low coupling (minimal dependencies - only needs DrawingSupplier for auto-population).

suggestions.name: PieChartConfiguration
suggestions.rationale: These members form a cohesive configuration unit that encapsulates all visual and behavioral settings for pie chart rendering. Extracting them into a PieChartConfiguration class improves separation of concerns by isolating configuration management from chart rendering logic, while providing a reusable configuration object that can be shared across multiple pie chart instances.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
The main responsibility shared by these methods is managing the complete configuration state of a pie chart plot. The getLabelLinkStyle() and setLabelLinkStyle() methods handle one specific aspect of configuration, while the hashCode() method computes a hash based on ALL configuration fields. Together, they represent the complete configuration state management for pie chart appearance and behavior.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
These members represent the "configuration" or "settings" domain of a pie chart. The extensive list of fields covers all aspects of pie chart appearance: visual styling (colors, fonts, strokes), layout properties (angles, gaps, offsets), labeling configuration, shadow effects, and behavioral settings. This is a cohesive configuration object that encapsulates all customizable aspects of pie chart rendering.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Separates configuration management from chart rendering logic
- Code reusability: Configuration can be shared, copied, or applied to multiple pie charts
- Complexity reduction: Removes ~40 configuration fields from the main PiePlot class
- Testability: Configuration logic can be tested independently, and different configurations can be easily created for testing

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to create a Configuration Object or Settings Object. This is a common design pattern where complex configuration state is extracted into a dedicated class that encapsulates all related settings and provides methods for managing them as a cohesive unit.

suggestions.name: PieLabelLinkRenderer
suggestions.rationale: These members form a cohesive unit responsible for rendering the visual links between pie chart labels and their corresponding slices. They encapsulate both the styling configuration and the complex geometric calculations needed to draw connecting lines, making them a natural candidate for extraction into a dedicated rendering component.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods share the responsibility of managing and rendering the visual links that connect pie chart labels to their corresponding pie slices. The getter/setter methods control the visibility and styling (paint, stroke) of these links, while the drawLeftLabel and drawRightLabel methods handle the actual geometric calculation and rendering of the link lines using different styles (standard, quad curve, cubic curve).

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
All members belong to the "label link rendering" domain within pie chart visualization. They encapsulate the complete lifecycle of label links - from configuration (visibility, styling) to geometric computation and drawing. The fields store the visual properties, while the methods use these properties to render connecting lines between pie slices and their labels.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates label link rendering logic from the broader pie chart plotting concerns
- Code reusability: The link rendering logic could be reused by other chart types that need similar label connections
- Complexity reduction: Removes complex geometric calculations and rendering logic from the main PiePlot class
- Testability: Label link rendering can be tested independently with mock graphics contexts and plot states

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate rendering concerns. The Strategy pattern could be applied here since different link styles (standard, quad curve, cubic curve) represent different rendering algorithms. This extraction creates a cohesive rendering component with high internal cohesion and low coupling to the main plot class.

suggestions.name: PieLabelRenderer
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive unit responsible for rendering simple labels in pie charts, including shadow effects, positioning, and visual styling. Extracting them creates a focused renderer that encapsulates all label drawing logic and configuration, separating presentation concerns from the main plot coordination logic.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods collectively handle the visual rendering and styling of simple labels in a pie chart. The getter/setter methods manage shadow offsets, label positioning, and shadow generation configuration, while the drawSimpleLabels method performs the actual rendering of labels with shadows, backgrounds, outlines, and text positioning.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
All members belong to the "label rendering" domain within the pie chart visualization system. They encapsulate the complete lifecycle of simple label presentation - from configuration (shadow offsets, positioning) to actual drawing operations (text rendering, shadow effects, background fills).

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates label rendering concerns from the main plot logic
- Code reusability: Label rendering logic could be reused by other chart types or plot variants
- Complexity reduction: Removes complex graphics rendering code from the main PiePlot class
- Testability: Label rendering can be tested independently with mock Graphics2D objects

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate rendering concerns. The Strategy pattern could be applied here, where different label rendering strategies (simple vs complex) could be implemented. This extraction supports the Single Responsibility Principle by creating a focused component responsible solely for label visualization.

suggestions.name: PieSectionRenderer
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive unit responsible for rendering individual pie chart sections, including their visual styling, shadows, and interactive features. Extracting them into a PieSectionRenderer class separates the complex drawing logic from the main plot coordination, improving maintainability and enabling different rendering strategies.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods collectively handle the visual rendering of individual pie chart sections. The main responsibility is drawing pie slices with their visual properties (colors, outlines, shadows) and managing interactive features (tooltips, URLs). The core method `drawItem()` orchestrates the rendering of a single pie section, while other methods provide configuration for visual appearance and interactivity.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
All members belong to the "pie section rendering" domain. They work together to transform data values into visual pie slices with proper geometry, styling, shadows, and interactive capabilities. The fields store rendering configuration (shadow properties, outline visibility, generators for tooltips/URLs), while methods handle the actual drawing logic and geometric calculations for individual sections.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates section rendering logic from overall plot management
- Code reusability: Section rendering logic could be reused by different pie plot types
- Complexity reduction: Removes complex drawing logic from the main PiePlot class
- Testability: Section rendering can be tested independently with mock graphics contexts

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate rendering concerns. The Strategy pattern could be applied here, where different section renderers could be plugged in. This extraction supports the Single Responsibility Principle by creating a focused class that only handles the visual representation of pie sections.

suggestions.name: PieLabelRenderer
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive unit responsible for rendering pie chart labels with consistent styling and intelligent positioning. They share label appearance properties and follow identical patterns for creating and drawing labels. Extracting them into a PieLabelRenderer separates rendering concerns from the main plot logic and improves maintainability.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods collectively handle the rendering and positioning of labels in a pie chart. They manage label appearance properties (font, paint, padding, gap), coordinate the distribution of labels to avoid overlaps, and execute the actual drawing of labels on both left and right sides of the pie chart. The core responsibility is transforming label data into visually positioned and styled text elements.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
All members belong to the "label rendering" domain within pie chart visualization. They share label styling properties, use the same label distributor for positioning, and follow identical patterns for creating TextBox objects with consistent styling. The drawLeftLabels and drawRightLabels methods contain nearly identical logic for label creation and positioning, differing only in geometric calculations.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates all label rendering concerns from the main plotting logic
- Code reusability: Label rendering logic can be reused by other chart types or pie chart variants
- Complexity reduction: Removes ~200 lines of label-specific code from the main PiePlot class
- Testability: Label rendering can be unit tested independently with mock graphics contexts

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate rendering concerns. The extracted class will have high cohesion (all methods work together to render labels) and low coupling (minimal dependencies beyond graphics context and chart state). It implements the Strategy pattern for label rendering, allowing different rendering approaches to be plugged in.

suggestions.name: PieChartConfiguration
suggestions.rationale: These members form a cohesive configuration unit that encapsulates all visual styling, behavioral settings, and generator properties for a pie chart. Extracting them into a PieChartConfiguration class improves separation of concerns by isolating configuration management from chart rendering logic, while providing a reusable configuration object that can be shared across multiple pie charts.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
The main responsibility shared by these methods is managing and comparing the complete configuration state of a PiePlot. The `equals` method performs comprehensive comparison of all visual and behavioral properties, while the getter/setter methods manage specific configuration values. Together, they handle the complete state representation and equality semantics of the pie chart configuration.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
These members represent the "configuration" or "settings" domain of a pie chart. They encapsulate all the visual styling properties (colors, fonts, strokes), behavioral settings (rotation, gaps, visibility flags), and generators (labels, tooltips, URLs). This forms a cohesive configuration object that defines how a pie chart should appear and behave.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Separates configuration management from chart rendering logic
- Code reusability: Configuration can be shared, copied, or applied to multiple pie charts
- Complexity reduction: The main PiePlot class becomes focused on rendering rather than property management
- Testability: Configuration logic can be tested independently from rendering logic
- Maintainability: All related configuration properties are grouped together

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to create a Value Object that encapsulates related data and behavior. The configuration represents a cohesive set of properties that are frequently accessed together and define the complete visual and behavioral state of a pie chart. This separation follows the Single Responsibility Principle by isolating configuration concerns.

suggestions.name: PieLegendRenderer
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive unit responsible for rendering pie chart legends, including paint management, label generation, and legend item creation. Extracting them into a PieLegendRenderer separates legend-specific concerns from core pie plotting logic, improving maintainability and enabling reuse across different chart contexts.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods collectively manage the visual representation and metadata generation for pie chart legend items. They handle section paint management (getting, setting, clearing section paints), legend label generation (including tooltips and URLs), and the creation of complete legend item collections with all visual and interactive properties.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
All members belong to the "legend rendering" domain within the pie chart visualization system. They work together to transform raw pie chart data into formatted, styled legend entries that can be displayed to users. The fields store generators, paint maps, and configuration needed for legend creation, while the methods orchestrate the legend item creation process.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates legend-specific logic from general pie plot concerns
- Code reusability: Legend creation logic could be reused by other chart types or legend variants
- Complexity reduction: Removes legend complexity from the main PiePlot class, making it more focused on core plotting
- Testability: Legend generation can be tested independently with mock data and configurations

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate the "legend creation" concern from the main plotting logic. The extracted class would have high cohesion (all methods work toward legend generation) and low coupling (minimal dependencies on the parent plot class through well-defined interfaces).

suggestions.name: PieChartStyleConfiguration
suggestions.rationale: These members form a cohesive styling configuration unit that manages all visual appearance properties of a pie chart including paints, strokes, and shapes for sections, labels, shadows, and legends. Extracting them into a dedicated configuration class separates visual styling concerns from core plotting logic, improving maintainability and enabling style reusability across different chart instances.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods collectively manage the visual styling and appearance properties of a pie chart. They handle getting/setting various Paint objects (colors/textures) for different visual elements like sections, shadows, labels, and legend items, along with Shape objects for legend items. The serialization methods ensure these visual properties can be persisted and restored.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
All members belong to the "visual styling" or "appearance configuration" domain. They represent a cohesive set of properties that define how the pie chart looks - colors, paints, shapes, and visual effects. This is essentially a configuration object that encapsulates all styling-related state.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Separates visual styling concerns from plotting logic
- Code reusability: Styling configuration could be shared across different plot types or saved as themes
- Complexity reduction: The main PiePlot class becomes more focused on core plotting behavior
- Testability: Visual styling logic can be tested independently from plotting algorithms
- Maintainability: All appearance-related changes are localized to one class

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate concerns. It creates a Value Object or Configuration Object that encapsulates related data and behavior. The extracted class will have high cohesion (all members relate to visual appearance) and low coupling (minimal dependencies beyond basic Java types).

suggestions.name: PiePlotConfiguration
suggestions.rationale: These members form a cohesive configuration management unit responsible for initializing and maintaining all pie plot settings and default values. Extracting them into a PiePlotConfiguration class separates configuration concerns from rendering logic, improving maintainability and allowing the configuration to be reused or tested independently.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods and fields are responsible for initializing and managing the configuration state of a PiePlot. The constructor methods set up all the default values for visual appearance, data handling, labeling, and behavioral properties. The getMinimumArcAngleToDraw() and setMinimumArcAngleToDraw() methods manage one specific configuration parameter. Together, they handle the complete initialization and basic configuration management of pie plot settings.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
These members represent the "configuration" or "settings" domain of a pie chart. They encapsulate all the default values, initialization logic, and basic property management that defines how a pie plot should behave and appear. This includes visual styling (colors, fonts, strokes), behavioral settings (rotation, gaps, angles), and feature toggles (auto-population flags, visibility settings).

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Separates configuration management from rendering and data processing logic
- Code reusability: Configuration logic could be reused for different plot types or chart variants
- Complexity reduction: Removes lengthy initialization code from the main PiePlot class
- Testability: Configuration logic can be tested independently from complex plotting algorithms
- Maintainability: All default values and initialization logic are centralized in one place

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate configuration concerns from the main plotting functionality. It implements the Single Responsibility Principle by isolating all initialization and basic configuration management into a cohesive unit. This also supports the Builder or Factory pattern for creating properly configured plot instances.

suggestions.name: PieDataExploder
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive unit responsible for managing pie chart data and calculating explosion effects for individual sections. They handle dataset lifecycle management, explosion percentage storage/retrieval, and the complex geometric calculations needed to position exploded pie sections, making them a natural candidate for extraction into a specialized component.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods collectively manage the pie chart's data source and the visual explosion effects of pie sections. They handle dataset lifecycle (getting/setting with proper listener management), manage explosion percentages for individual sections, and coordinate the rendering process including shadow effects and arc positioning calculations.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
The methods represent the "pie section data and explosion management" domain. They work together to maintain the relationship between data keys and their visual representation, particularly focusing on how sections can be "exploded" (visually separated) from the main pie. The explosion functionality is tightly coupled with dataset operations since explosion percentages are keyed by dataset keys.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Separates data/explosion management from other plotting concerns like styling, labeling, and legends
- Code reusability: The explosion calculation logic could be reused by other pie-based visualizations
- Complexity reduction: Removes complex explosion mathematics and dataset listener management from the main plot class
- Testability: Explosion calculations and dataset operations can be tested independently

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate the "data model with explosion effects" concern from the broader plotting responsibilities. The explosion feature is a cohesive unit that depends heavily on dataset keys and requires complex geometric calculations that form a natural boundary for extraction.

suggestions.name: SectionPaintResolver
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive unit responsible for resolving and adapting Paint objects for pie chart sections. They handle the complex logic of paint lookup with auto-population and geometric adaptation for gradient paints, representing a distinct responsibility that can be cleanly separated from the main plotting logic.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods collectively handle the lookup and dynamic adjustment of Paint objects for pie chart sections. The main responsibility is to retrieve the appropriate Paint for a section and adapt it based on the rendering context (particularly for RadialGradientPaint objects that need geometric adjustments).

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
These members represent the "section paint resolution" domain within pie chart rendering. They encapsulate the logic for determining how individual pie sections should be painted, including automatic population behavior and context-sensitive paint adjustments based on the current pie geometry.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates paint lookup and adaptation logic from other pie plotting concerns
- Code reusability: Paint resolution logic could be reused by other chart types or pie plot variants
- Complexity reduction: Removes paint-specific logic from the main PiePlot class, making it more focused on core plotting
- Testability: Paint resolution logic can be tested independently with mock geometries and states

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate the concern of paint resolution from the broader pie plotting responsibilities. The Strategy pattern could be applied here, where different paint resolution strategies could be plugged in based on chart requirements.

suggestions.name: PieRenderingConfiguration
suggestions.rationale: These methods and fields form a cohesive unit responsible for configuring how pie chart sections are rendered, positioned, and labeled. Extracting them into a configuration class separates rendering concerns from core plotting logic, improving maintainability and allowing the configuration to be reused or tested independently.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods collectively manage the configuration and rendering behavior of pie chart sections. They control how pie slices are positioned (startAngle, direction), which data values are included in the visualization (ignoreNullValues, ignoreZeroValues), and how labels are drawn and positioned around the pie chart. The main responsibility is configuring the visual presentation and layout of pie chart elements.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
These members represent the "pie chart rendering configuration" domain. They form a cohesive unit that determines how pie sections are displayed, oriented, and labeled. The fields work together to define the visual presentation rules, while the methods either expose these configurations or use them to perform rendering operations like label positioning and mouse wheel rotation handling.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Separates rendering configuration from other plot concerns like data management or event handling
- Code reusability: The configuration could be reused across different pie plot implementations or shared between similar chart types
- Complexity reduction: Removes rendering-specific logic from the main PiePlot class, making it more focused on core plotting functionality
- Testability: Rendering configuration logic can be tested independently without requiring the full plot infrastructure

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate configuration concerns from the main plotting logic. It creates a cohesive configuration object that encapsulates related rendering parameters and behaviors, following the principle of high cohesion within the extracted class while reducing coupling in the original PiePlot class.

suggestions.name: PieLayoutCalculator
suggestions.rationale: These members form a cohesive unit responsible for calculating the geometric layout of pie chart elements. They work together to determine spacing, positioning, and the various rectangular areas needed for proper pie chart rendering. Extracting them separates complex spatial calculations from the main plotting logic, improving maintainability and testability.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods collectively handle the geometric layout and spatial calculations for rendering a pie chart. They manage interior gaps, circular constraints, label spacing, and most importantly, the complex mathematical calculations in drawPie() that determine the various rectangular areas (link area, explode area, pie area) based on spacing parameters and circular constraints.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
All members belong to the "geometric layout" domain of pie chart rendering. They share spatial configuration properties (interiorGap, circular, maximumLabelWidth, simpleLabels) and work together to calculate the nested rectangular regions that define where pie sections and labels are positioned. The drawPie() method orchestrates these calculations using the configuration properties.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates complex geometric calculations from rendering logic
- Reduces Complexity: The main PiePlot class becomes simpler by removing intricate area calculations
- Enhances Testability: Geometric calculations can be unit tested independently
- Improves Reusability: Layout logic could potentially be reused for other circular chart types

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate concerns. The geometric layout calculations represent a cohesive mathematical subsystem that can be encapsulated. This creates high cohesion (all methods work together for spatial layout) and reduces coupling by isolating complex calculations from the main plotting logic.

suggestions.name: SectionOutlineStrokeAutoPopulator
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive unit responsible for managing the automatic population of section outline strokes in pie charts. They encapsulate the configuration and lookup logic for this specific auto-population behavior, which represents a distinct responsibility that can be separated from the main plotting functionality.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods collectively manage the automatic population behavior for section outline strokes in a pie chart. They provide a getter/setter pair for the autoPopulateSectionOutlineStroke boolean flag and a lookup method that uses this flag to determine how to retrieve section outline strokes.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
These members represent the "auto-population configuration" domain concept specifically for section outline strokes. This is a cohesive subsystem that controls whether the plot should automatically populate missing section outline stroke values, which is a specific rendering behavior configuration.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates auto-population logic from the main plotting concerns
- Code reusability: This auto-population pattern could be reused for other visual properties
- Complexity reduction: Removes configuration management details from the main PiePlot class
- Testability: Auto-population behavior can be tested independently

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate configuration concerns from rendering logic. The extracted class would represent a Strategy pattern for auto-population behavior, encapsulating the decision-making logic for when and how to populate missing visual properties.

suggestions.name: SectionOutlinePaintConfigurator

They encapsulate the logic for determining when and how to auto-populate paint values, representing a single responsibility that can be extracted to reduce the complexity of the main PiePlot class.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods collectively manage the automatic population behavior for section outline paint in pie charts. They provide a getter/setter pair for the autoPopulateSectionOutlinePaint boolean flag and a lookup method that uses this flag to determine how to retrieve outline paint for pie sections.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
These members represent the "auto-population configuration" domain concept specifically for section outline paint. This is a cohesive subsystem that controls whether the plot should automatically generate outline paint values for sections that don't have explicitly defined colors.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates auto-population logic from the main plotting concerns
- Code reusability: This configuration pattern could be reused for other auto-population features (stroke, fill paint, etc.)
- Complexity reduction: Removes configuration management details from the main PiePlot class
- Testability: Auto-population behavior can be tested in isolation

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate configuration concerns from core plotting functionality. It represents a Strategy pattern component that encapsulates the decision-making logic for paint lookup based on auto-population settings.
